items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.q: When you flex your elbow, which muscle is the agonist?
items.why: Students confuse agonist (the prime mover doing the action) with antagonist (the opposing muscle that relaxes). In elbow flexion, biceps is the agonist; triceps is the antagonist (and vice versa for extension).

items.q: Which PED is most likely used by a 100 m sprinter for muscle gain?
items.why: It's Anabolic steroid. “Beta blocker” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly how many minutes should a thorough warm-up last?
items.why: Exact answer: 10. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
